"Concurrently, the process of critiquing and rating wine must include balance, complexity and representation of place as metrics, all of which are affected by the winemaker’s decisions; the monolithic style displayed in several wines within this report often emphasizes homogeneity and noticeable alcoholic burn at the expense of balance, nuance and a sense of origin.

posts.name: Ben M a n d l e r
posts.cooked:
I appreciate this perspective. I don’t agree with it, really, but I think it’s good to have a diversity of perspectives in the world of wine criticism. Makes it easier to find a palate and philosophy you’re aligned with. If a wine has six reviews from major critics, all 92-94 without much helpful qualitative description, it’s hard to know what to make of it.

I’ve never had a SQN wine - too dear for me and not popular in my circles - but I think that the idea of representation of place in California winemaking is mostly (mostly!) made up, at least in terms of the claims made by critics, consumers, and, to a lesser or greater extent, winemakers. The geology and climate are so variable over such small distances, and the viticultural and winemaking practices so variable between wineries - and sometimes within an individual winery - that it is extraordinarily difficult to tease out what “representation of place” means. Reading reviews, both professional and not, when I hear people talking about a wine speaking of its place, it usually seems to just mean that they like it a lot, that it gives them a sense of place. They are projecting the feeling of having been spoken to onto the wine, but really they are talking about themselves. I know this isn’t a very popular opinion, but I’ve not yet been convinced otherwise. So when people talk about wanting a sense of place, I sympathize with them, but I think the idea is a bit precious here, most of the time.

posts.name: Ben M a n d l e r
posts.cooked:
I subscribe to the concept of terroir everywhere. I just think that the terroir in CA is so much more complicated than it is in, say, Burgundy or Bordeaux, and we’ve had so few generations of vines planted in most vineyards, that understanding any “signature” of a vineyard, or especially a region, can be a messy thing. There are certainly vineyards that I think have characteristic signatures, but even on the individual vineyard level it’s a tricky business. In some cases the character is due to the effects of nearby vegetation, like California bay. In some cases it’s due to the effects of nearby livestock operations. In some cases it’s due more to the specific vineyard practices than anything intrinsic about the site. In some cases it’s due to being extremely virused and so not ripening “properly”, well that will definitely change when the vineyard is replanted.

I think the clearest senses of site are found in the vineyards that are the most singularly located. Halcòn is a good example - a spectacular site for Syrah, to be sure, and its strength of identity is helped by not having a lot of other vineyards nearby. That makes it easier to tease out a sense of place distinct from other vineyards, much moreso than, say, the Middle Reach of the Russian River Valley.

Anyway, really my intended complaint here is not about whether or not terroir exists, but about the consumer/critic’s approval of a “sense of place” being somehow properly or accurately conveyed. Which I think says a lot more about the preferences of the drinker than the actual terroir of the vineyard.

And yes, to your last question, I think the concept of a sense of place specific to, say, the Russian River Valley, is pretty much complete nonsense. Very easy to taste a bunch of ripe Pinot brought up in Francois Freres and think that that is the signature of the Russian River Valley, but if it is it’s primarily because of the human aspects of terroir, not the geographic ones.

posts.cooked:
Jeff_Leve:
Ben_M_a_n_d_l_e_r:

I just think that the terroir in CA is so much more complicated than it is in, say, Burgundy or Bordeaux

Why would say that ?

Coastal California has an enormously more complicated and variable geology, climate, and topography than either Burgundy or Bordeaux, due to the fact that it is a tectonically active region immediately adjacent to the cold Pacific. Maritime Atlantic France is so chill compared to California. As someone who grew up in London and now lives in Santa Rosa, I can hardly explain to my friends back in the UK how much more extreme the variations are here. They almost don’t believe me.

Geologically, Bordeaux and Burgundy are both mostly fairly simple mixes of variably calcareous sediments. People spend a lot of time finely dividing them up and amplifying their differences, but they represent a pretty small segment of the total geologic variability a region can have. On the other hand, coastal California is an absolute mess. It’s such a mess that its geologic diversity hasn’t even been properly mapped. Within an area much smaller than Burgundy you can have metamorphic serpentinites with such high magnesium that they’re toxic to much plant growth, thick volcanic series, chill sandstones, and heavy clay. Bedrock porosity and soil thickness vary a lot too, from almost untenable clay to essentially pure sand so free-draining it’s hard to plant in, and from zero soil to pretty thick stuff, again over small distances.

Climatically, compare going the 30 miles from St Estephe to Pomerol, then compare that from going from the Petaluma Gap to Dry Creek Valley, or from Santa Maria to the Happy Canyon. The amount of climatic variability over the same distance is not comparable. Peak temperature variation across the major viticultural areas of Bordeaux tends to be on the order of a few degrees, while peak temperatures in coastal California viticultural areas can vary by 20-30 degrees over the same distance.

Topographically, Bordeaux is basically flat compared to coastal CA - the highest point on St Emilion isn’t even 400 feet. Burgundy has a total elevation range of maybe 500 feet, You can see triple that over barely a couple miles on Mt Veeder or the Santa Cruz mtns. And unlike Burgundy, where the vineyards have predominantly southwest to east aspects, in coastal California anything goes.

Add into that the fact that California has a lot of variation in irrigation whereas Burgundy and Bordeaux basically have none.

So it’s a lot more complicated.

Geologically the Bordeaux region is located in the Mesozoic intra-cratonic Aquitaine Basin. The underlying geology (bedrock) varies slowly over scales of 10 of kms. California is a tectonically active plate boundary that is made up of a very diverse series of accretionary terranes. The geology can vary wildly over a distance of 100s of metres.

There is simply no comparison geologically in terms of complexity and variability. California is probably 2 or 3 orders of magnitude more geologically complex than the Bordeaux region. This is observable at every scale.

Of course terroir is much more than geology and I am not trying to imply otherwise. This is why Ben referred to climate, aspect and topography as well as geology.

yes he is right and he reasons like a geologist. The areas where tectonic movements have and are strong are obviously more diversified in rock types and subsoils.

Because the complexity of a wine is not only due to the subsoil (mother rock) as geologists imagine, but it comes from the soil (which comes from the decomposition of the mother rock under the long-term action of the climate, micro-organisms and vegetation), the climate (the vintage - short-term action), the vine and the action of man.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Thanks for everyone who has posted thoughtful comments on this. Especially for everyone who has taken the time to read Matthew’s clear but nuanced report, which I think contains the answers to most of the other questions raised here. He may jump in if he has anything to add, but I just wanted to make two remarks.

The first is that we certainly don’t take lightly the issue of “downgrading” a producer—or rather, a producer’s wines—in the context of how they have previously been reviewed. As @CFu astutely points out, it’s very much like how I imagine our colleagues at the Michelin Guide must feel. It would be far more agreeable to hand out very high scores to the same producers every year. We’re conscious of how much work goes into making wine, and how a legacy with TWA can be an important part of a winery’s story over the last few decades. And it does indeed involve internal discussion. However, we believe that for wine criticism to merit its name, and to have any enduring relevance for consumers, we need to reevaluate wines and wineries afresh, critically, each and every time we review them.

The second remark is how pleased I am with Matthew’s work for TWA so far. He has not shied away from some brave calls, whether with his Monte Bello review backed up with lab analysis, or the way he has expressed himself, I think very frankly but also respectfully, regarding SQN. He is actually a few months my senior, as we are fellow '89s (I am certainly not against hiring someone younger than me for a permanent position, but I haven’t yet managed it). With over a decade at Mélisse under his belt and a very sharp palate, as well as his stint at Decanter, I think his credentials are robust. But one of the things I admire most is his willingness to learn, evolve and refine as a taster: there’s a difference between simple repetition of an activity and real experience, and Matthew’s approach means that he has built up plenty of the latter.

posts.cooked:
That was indeed enough to provoke something of a minor rebellion…

That was six years ago now, and we were a lot less far down the path of figuring out what should be part of our publication’s heritage and what should be part of its history.

I think Matt has published a critical review in line what many of the WB community has been quietly thinking for some time, but were too timid to put out there. Especially as the allure of turbocharged ripening practices waned in many parts of the world (Pavie?) and a more restrained profile of alcohol/tannin/concentration have taken hold, balance is seen as a higher virtue. What will be fascinating to watch is the possibility of re definition of style in certain producers. Will they continue to stubbornly produce high alcohol , high concentration, intense wines? I give the WA full marks for once again be the publication that leads, and strives for relevance in the crowded world of wine reviewers.

What will be fascinating to watch is the possibility of re definition of style in certain producers. Will they continue to stubbornly produce high alcohol , high concentration, intense wines? Or will the market re-evaluate their status and drift away from a cult like devotion to the winemakers? I give the WA full marks for once again be the publication that leads, and strives for relevance in the crowded world of wine reviewers.

Dennis,

Interesting insights - and though there certainly are a number of folks on this board who do not like riper style wines, my guess is that there are an equal number of folks that do - and really do not care what scores these wines get.

I think the challenge is for newer producers that do not have the track record of some of these ‘legacy brands’ producing riper style wines. How will they be perceived?

And remember that Matt is not covering Napa, home to plenty of uber ripe wines too. Not sure who is covering those wines, but I’ve yet to see anyone willing to take the same stance there and yearn for wines that show more ‘sense of place’.

Will wineries alter their style of winemaking? My guess is not so much - it’s clear that there is no one reviewer that has the ‘pull’ that some had in the past.

Curious to hear other takes on this though.

Cheers.

Well, this has been an interesting thread and certainly popular. As a huge fan of your (Adam’s) wines and greatly appreciative of your tasting reviews and thoughts, I think this is a good place to opine.

  1. Good for Matt. I think this type of review is spot on and exactly what is needed in the world of wine reviews. Thoughtful, relative, unflinching, yet appreciative of what may not be his personal tastes.

  2. What if a wine expresses its terroir and it sucks? I think there is great artistry in expressing terroir beautifully and that has been and should be one ideal, but ultimately, is it not about the sip and what is makes you feel? What if a totally manipulated wine from many vineyards is just plain tasty? Is that wrong if you enjoy it? SQN, The Third Twin, Next of Kin, Fingers Crossed and Antica Terra do not seem to be trying to express terroir (I don’t see it if they are). Is that inherently wrong if they are delicious?

  3. If you don’t like that style, Matt’s review is that much more illuminating. Cherish the review where its words (not score), guide your decisions well, whether buying or passing.

  4. I think my tastes have changed over time, and I can’t say that my favorite wine I’ve had will be my favorite wine next year (if consumed as it was). But, the best two wines I have ever had (to date) are a SQN Heart Chorea 2002 (enjoyed in 2019) and a Harlan Estate 2006 (enjoyed in 2020). I’ve had a number of board favorites that push those wines and are completely different in style, but if I am being honest, those two wines are the best I have ever tried. The SQN HC reminded me of the the best Northern Rhone wines I have had+ and clearly (at least for me), that wine aged so well.

  5. I have not had young SQN, but I have had young Next of Kin, The Third Twin, and Fingers Crossed. For me, these are not great wines yet, but you can tell they have a lot of stuffing. Nothing like the 2002 HC, but not hard to imagine they could be similar with cellar time. My hope is they will mature like the SQN.

  6. It may turn out that by the time the NOK, TTT and FC are in their prime, I have moved on and I don’t enjoy them. Individual tastes differ and change and that is the beauty of exploring the world of wine. Good for the honest reviewer (especially those with a refreshing viewpoint like Matt) . Making great wine, no matter the definition, may be influenced by the critic and rating, but I doubt it’s sustaining if the consumer does not like the juice you make.

If you don’t like the wines I like, may you enjoy your favorites as much as I mine – and perhaps, the experience we share together will make for a great memory and bridge the difference.

posts.name: Matthew Luczy
posts.cooked:
Well, if there was ever a time to write my first WineBerserkers comment, this is probably it!

Thank you all for posting your thoughts. It is an exciting and important discussion and, in my view, one long overdue in the landscape of wine criticism. For years and even decades now, we’ve lived in an ecosystem where one could guess a wine’s style by its score with alarming - and even annoying - accuracy, leaving many to wonder if other viewpoints would ever gain a platform to move the needle in a different direction.

As I made clear in my report, I have nothing in principle against flashy, ripe wines, those who make them, or those who enjoy them. That said, such styles often slip the leash on the scoring scale, many times while displaying qualities that should, in my opinion, disqualify them from entering the upper ranges. This doesn’t mean that riper wines should be precluded from scoring well by default - lest we end up with an inverted version of the same problem as before - but delineation is necessary. Otherwise, rating systems and reviewers aren’t doing the consumer any favors, much less providing a service worth paying for.

Regarding Saxum: it’s tempting and understandable to stylistically lump these wines in with Sine Qua Non, but I feel there are substantive differences between them. Saxum’s wines aren’t forced or pushed into the ripeness levels they attain. Instead, they reflect the sites they come from, mostly in relatively cooler, calcareous sections of an otherwise warm region that more naturally produces wines of such stature. Much of Justin Smith’s focus in the vineyards and the winery is on retaining balance and vibrancy, which is clear to me in the finished wines. The ripeness goes to 11, but everything else goes to 11 alongside it: the body, power and punch, as well as the acidity, length and nuance, resulting in ripe, full-throttle wines that are still harmonious.

Look at the other 6 pages of SQN listings. Distentia wines are almost cheaper now at auction. 17th nail is as much about the package, I would humbly submit, as the contents of the bottle. Manfred is possibly a better artist than a winemaker. And I think he is a helluva winemaker in many respects!

I sometimes wonder if Bourbon and spirits have the same issue to wrestle with. Barrel strength and extremely high alcohol content spirits also pack a large flavor punch, but is it at the expense of complexity?

Beer as well, Dennis.

I think the movement we saw over the past 20 to 25 years with bolder, richer, riper flavors, doctored up with lavish use of Oak, often resulting in higher alcohol, has permeated the entire industry. I have a hard time drinking whiskeys that are above 50%. One of my favorite every day drinking scotches is at 42%. Even the berserker cognac Cuvee lot 85 clocked in at 52.3% and showed every bit of it, it was tasty, but I had to add an ice cube, I know scandalous. Beer has been one of the worst culprits, seriously, pushing the envelope on doctored up flavors and very high alcohol

I can’t disagree with the commentary on people becoming enamored with over-oaked messes, but I do disagree that barrel strength spirits have any sort of relationship with wine as discussed here. Spirits aren’t always ruined by and are sometimes designed to be something we can supplement with water to get the perfect custom experience. I’ve tasted far more spirits that, in my opinion, were ruined by coming in at a diluted 40-45% with no complexity, than spirits that are 50+% and unsaveable (if too hot) with a little water. Bruisers can always be diluted.

I’ll qualify that with a note that, indeed there are many makers who release big number cask strength spirits for the sake of the number, but those are bad makers. Some spirits, stylistically or method-wise are just lower alcohol content to begin with, and that’s neither a bad or good thing when released at cask strength. Where good whisky is ruined by coming in at low alcohol percentages is where there is an economic calculation to sell more (watered down), but there is certainly great whisky sold diluted. To me, it’s the exception rather than the rule as they tend to be less complex.

posts.name: Marcu$ Stanley
posts.cooked:
In principle, I agree with everything Matt says here but it’s still hard for me to grasp what qualities in a “big” wine make it “harmonious” and “balanced” and what “pushed” actually means in practice when you taste it. Also, how SQN gets a 93-95 point rating if (implicitly based on the description) it lacks harmony and distinctiveness beyond monolithic size, or what harmony and distinctiveness it does have to earn that rating.

It feels like Matthew looking to make a kind of political statement speaking for TWA as an arbiter of wine taste and sort of officially update TWA’s approach to move it beyond remaining vestiges of Parkerism. So it’s not just a review of SQN but an official pronouncement on “wines of size”. But that quality sort of forces a kind of caution and almost fuzziness. There’s been a lot of frank writing over the years on SQN where various critics have been blunt in their immediate reaction to it, and those frankly critical writings often seem more precise about what the issues are or aren’t with the wine. For example, Julia Harding writing in 2012 gave what felt to me like a very thoughtful balanced assessment of SQN that just tracked her reaction and as a non-SQN drinker helped me understand why the wine has so many enthusiastic fans and so many detractors:

with these wines it was an internal dilemma – did I love them or hate them? What surprised me the most was the intense, individual fragrance of each wine. I expected the wines to be massive and not very expressive whereas I found them massive on the palate but very expressive on the nose and sometimes that perfume carried through on the palate.

The big problem for me was the alcohol level. Not just the heat on the finish on some – but not all – of the wines, but the effect of the alcohol. I have a fairly low alcohol tolerance in general but even though I was spitting rather than drinking, I could feel the alcohol creeping into my system and exerting pressure across my forehead. I think I could only ever drink these wines in very small quantities – but some of them had so much complexity that a small glass would be quite enough for an evening’s entertainment. I find it hard to imagine drinking them with food, however.

After all this musing, I rated the best of these wines – in some vintages the Syrah dominant-wine and in some years the Grenache-dominant wine – much more highly than I would have expected to do given their reputation for mass over elegance. They may not be not the kind of wines I would seek out… because they are certainly not refreshing, but I could see a certain beauty in them, at least until the alcohol clouded my view.

Anyway, it’s tough for me as a drinker who sometimes does like “big” wines, but more often doesn’t, to pinpoint what makes a good “big” wine, so I sympathize with the dilemma. For me when I don’t like a “big” wine it usually has to do with some combination of high alcohol and a heavy and oppressively unrefreshing quality that tires me out and makes the wine quickly become boring, one-note or weird flavors that are initially striking/interesting but rapidly grow off-putting, etc. Jamie Goode has referred to “dead fruit” which is a descriptor that resonates for me.

To me Tertre Roteboeuf is an interesting example of a (very) big wine that I can enjoy despite some qualities that I sometimes find off-putting. For one thing, it tends to be extremely sweet. But it can also have a subtle lift and flavor complexity. It’s not a wine I would drink every night but I really enjoy it at times.

This comment feels right to me… I don’t think the targeting of a sacred cow like SQN is either accidental or coincidental. It feels like an intentional (subversive) act orchestrated to reorient the publication away from the heritage inculcated by the publication’s patriarch.

IMO, the potential risk is in that an increasing number of the current lineup of reviewers at TWA are unknown outside the ever dwindling number of cognoscenti and I’m not convinced that a reviewer’s impressions or scores are able to project beyond their anonymity. Therefore the dangers of reorientation are manifest in the long shadow cast by the patriarch and this will be an ever present risk until someone in the current lineup can emerge to assert comparable levels of commercial presence, credence, influence, and/or relevance. The challenge is that this typically isn’t accomplished through understatement, nuance, and balance - there’s a fair amount of bombast, bluster, and braggadocio that’s required to shake people out of their doldrums and sit up and take notice. Maybe making SQN an early target was an example of that notion.

I may be a contrarian on this point but I found the SQN scores to be excessively punitive. Most can overlook a simple difference of opinion, however contemptuousness can completely disrupt any attempt at fostering goodwill or establishing credibility. IMO, it felt like someone who hates jazz had been assigned to write a free jazz review of one of free jazz’s most popular practitioners.

posts.name: Ted Hase
posts.cooked:
I’m spending way too much time on this subject but I owe you a response. I think Matthew is an honest, serious, dedicated, and devoted wine professional and consequently it’s hard for me to imagine that he’s approaches what he does in his professional domain frivolously. If we accept that, we have to accept that his selection of SQN as a subject of review was intentional and that he was well aware of the controversy it could raise. If his intent create a cause celebre and attack, big, brutishly extracted, high alcohol wines, there are a plethora of candidates for him to use as softer examples setting himself up for dropping the hammer after his predilections are more widely understood and embraced. But he picked SQN and I say “good for him” and I honestly celebrate his audacity!

However, (again IMO) it didn’t feel like he was criticizing the wines; rather it felt more like he was criticizing the winemaking. Now I’m not saying that this is a taboo subject, rather I’m saying that it should be done individually and not as a package deal. Was it really necessary to create a review that bundled Manfred and all of his “disciples” into a tidy package implying that there’s a Svengali-like influence producing winemakers like Bill Walsh produces head coaches. And much like Bill Walsh, all of these Manfred-clones are all producing derivative “West Coast Offense wines”.

Admittedly, he did a good job of reorienting us toward a new “North Star” but, in doing so I felt like he performed a disservice to TWA and TWA readers who who don’t share his sensibilities and who now have to reconcile TWA’s 2021 guidance of a (98-100) with an upward potential with a more inconclusive 95 which in this case just happens to be half the distance between 90 and 100, or the best of a bunch of wines he doesn’t like.

As to your second point, he’s not tilting at SQN per se, SQN happens to be the face of an entire category of wines that follow the philosophy of the patriarch of the publication. However, even on this front he patronizes a bit in suggesting that “fans of the style” back the truck up and buy all they’re allowed. In the case of the 2021 Distentia III Grenache, based on all of his data points, one could infer he’s “recommending” that folks spend $290/bottle to buy a wine he rates in the middle of his grading curve, and that has a six year drinking window. Personally, for high dollar wines, I do think there’s a psychological “line in the sand” that would preempt even the most eager buyers. I get the pandering, but it’s hard to think that fans of any style would back the truck up for a $300 wine that had six years of optimal life and that rated in the low 90s.

I don’t understand why it seems that professional wine reviewers feels the need to equivocate just to placate the dissenters of their opinion. It’s late, I’m tired, and I’m probably not making much sense so I’ll just leave it at that.

(Edit: Sorry this should have been a reply to @bretrooks but it just goes to show how tired I am. Apologies to both @Cris_Whetstone and @bretrooks. I don’t know how to redirect it.)

posts.name: Brian G r a f s t r o m
posts.cooked:
TWA’s “Internal Discussion” Room when a Sacred Cow gets a less high score than before:

William has chosen not to indicate which wines get the group pow-wow treatment, and which ones don’t, and that’s fine — he doesn’t have to. It’s clear that not all reviewed wines are treated equally at TWA. It’s clear that greater scrutiny is given to less-high-than-before scores than is given to other scores; it could also be said that there are additional hurdles a less-high-than-before score must sometimes clear before TWA will publish them.

That doesn’t sound like impartial reviewing to me. From my perspective, Impartiality in wine reviewing looks like this:

  • all wines/scores get equal amount of scrutiny;
  • either all wines get the Internal Discussion Treatment, or none do, or the few that do are literally chosen at random;
  • no thought or consideration is given to whether a winery has chosen for themselves to make a publication’s reviews “a part of their story”; and
  • no thought or consideration is given to prior reviews of prior vintages (sometimes even written by prior reviewers!); what person A said about wine X in vintage 1 should have no impact whatsoever on what person B says about wine X in vintage 7.

As soon as a wine is pulled aside from the group and given some or all of these extra considerations or extra treatments true and complete impartiality has left the building.

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:
I think some people misuse the word “impartial”; there is no indication in any post on this thread that there is any kind of undue influence on the WA because two of the reviewers may have, at some point, discussed a wine. (Questions of impartiality and Independence are best left to other publications, I think.) That certain wines are reviewed somewhat differently is also not an indication of a lack of impartiality; treating different things differently doesn’t make it unfair. I don’t think there’s a good reason to expect a mass produced negociant Bourgougne to be reviewed exactly the same way as the top Chevalier; it’s a waste of time and process (do I want William or Antonio Galloni to spend as much on the Bichot Bourgogne as he does on the Gibourg Clos Vougeot? Why?). As a much smarter man than I once said, a foolish consistency is the hobgoblin of little minds. What’s being described above isn’t impartiality, it’s a decontextualized double blind; that is not the same thing.

William can, of course, correct me if I misread him, but my understanding from my experience with reviewer publications is that there’s a basic editorial process at work. If one reviewer finds a surprising or unexpected issue at a producer, it wouldn’t be unusual for an editor to look in (often requested). That does not mean the editor will simply overrule the initial reviewer, but having two reviewers (especially if a more experienced reviewers is involved) is often better. To paraphrase a recent interview, wines don’t just fall out of coconut trees, they exist in context. For example, de Vogue showed well out of barrel for years - if a new reviewer gave it very high scores on that basis (it’s old vine Musigny!), it would be neither impartial (wrong word, but still) nor in any way inappropriate for an experienced reviewer or editor to suggest a reconsideration of the score on the basis that the wines never show as well from bottle.

I think there’s a certain level of cynicism from some people that has led them to think that wine scores are just designed to please producers, so they read any contextualized reviews in this way. Personally, I think context is good - otherwise reviews would be snapshots of barrel tastings, which are often not especially useful (in many cases these are not even finished wines!). But, even moreso, I think this thread shows that while many consumers claim they want producers to receive the low scores they deserve, it’s a lot more complicated. Those low scores, when given to a producers those consumers like, make those consumers quite unhappy! They like that producer, know that producer, have a cellar full of that producer’s wines and have been drinking those wines for years. They have effectively been told they have bad taste and their wines may not be worth that much - not what anyone wants to hear! So sure, everyone wants low scores, but for producers they don’t like. I think this as much as anything else leads to a certain smoothing in scores, rather than the fear of getting blacklisted (what’s one domaine/chateau?) or advertising revenue. This thread now has so many posts, and SQN still received a 95! The horror

matthewluczy:

Much of my perspective on drinking windows and ageability comes from my 2013-2023 tenure at Mélisse. Flights of mid-to-late 2000s vintages of Sine Qua Non were featured at several wine dinners during this time, organized by collectors and often served in the context of wines such as 50s Barolo, 60s Burgundy, 70s Napa Cabernet, 80s Bordeaux, etc. These comparisons provided invaluable insights into the performance of numerous styles of wine over time.

My experiences with aged Sine Qua Non mirror those many posted here: the wines show negligible development in secondary notes, and their alcohol levels tend to become more prominent, masking their clarity. Given such results, and the fact that they tend to show all their cards on release if you like the style, I find it difficult to see the upside to aging wines of this style beyond the near term.

When it comes to Rhys, we indeed have an apples-to-oranges comparison: these are mountain wines with true structural tenacity tempered by impeccable balance. Progress over the last 15 years has been relentless. This causes me to be quite optimistic about their capacity to gain in interest and complexity over time.

Just want to say thanks for jumping back in here - you certainly don’t owe anyone explanations but what you have provided should prove of value to all.

Cheers
